edges.description: Ingested parasites establish non-invasive colonization at the mucosal surface.
edges.target: Trophozoite colonization of the proximal small-intestinal epithelial surface

edges.description: Colonizing trophozoites initiate epithelial injury programs that vary by infecting assemblage.
edges.source: Trophozoite colonization of the proximal small-intestinal epithelial surface

edges.description: Trophozoite-triggered epithelial injury activates caspase-3-dependent apoptosis.
Trophozoite-triggered epithelial injury induces tight-junction disruption.
Increased epithelial apoptosis contributes to loss of mucosal barrier integrity.
Tight-junction injury is associated with reduced claudin-1 expression.
Tight-junction injury contributes to reduced epithelial resistance.
Claudin-1 loss contributes to impaired epithelial barrier function.
Hypoxia-dependent PKC signaling changes exacerbate permeability defects.
Barrier pathology is accompanied by reduced Na-glucose absorption.
Barrier pathology is accompanied by increased secretory current.
Reduced sodium-glucose transport contributes to malabsorptive disease.
Increased epithelial secretory drive contributes to diarrheal output.
Mucus-layer perturbation is linked to altered commensal biofilm architecture.
Microbiota and epithelial barrier perturbation may reinforce nutrient malabsorption.
Fat malabsorption contributes to steatorrheal stool phenotype.
Prolonged malabsorption contributes to loss of body weight.
Severe pediatric nutritional compromise can present as failure to thrive.
Chronic giardiasis in poorly nourished settings can contribute to linear growth faltering.
In vulnerable pediatric populations, chronic giardiasis-associated nutritional disruption may contribute to cognitive deficits.
A subset develops persistent post-infectious bowel dysfunction after acute gastroenteritis.

nodes.description: Host epithelial injury severity varies by Giardia assemblage and can intensify during mixed infections.

evidence.reference_title: Giardia duodenalis assemblage-specific induction of apoptosis and tight junction disruption in human intestinal epithelial cells: effects of mixed infections.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, infections by isolates in combination with isolates from another assemblage enhanced the epithelial disruption and apoptosis.

nodes.description: Giardia-induced epithelial injury triggers caspase-3-dependent enterocyte apoptosis.

evidence.reference_title: Giardia duodenalis assemblage-specific induction of apoptosis and tight junction disruption in human intestinal epithelial cells: effects of mixed infections.
evidence.snippet: All disruptions were caspase-3 dependent and were more pronounced when caused by a non-host specific assemblage.

nodes.description: Expression of the tight-junction protein claudin-1 is reduced in chronic human giardiasis.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of chronic Giardia lamblia infection on epithelial transport and barrier function in human duodenum.
evidence.snippet: As structural correlate, reduced claudin 1 expression and increased epithelial apoptosis were detected.

evidence.reference_title: Giardia intestinalis.
evidence.snippet: Further studies of giardiasis in poorly nourished children in developing regions supports an important contributing role of Giardia in stunting and cognitive impairment.

evidence.reference_title: The Gut-Wrenching Effects of Cryptosporidiosis and Giardiasis in Children.
evidence.snippet: Structural damages inflicted by both parasites to epithelial cells in the large and small intestines could severely impair children's gut health, including the ability to absorb nutrients, resulting in stunted growth, diminished neurocognitive development, and other long-term effects.

nodes.description: Giardia-associated barrier perturbation disrupts commensal microbiota organization.

evidence.reference_title: Interactions of Giardia sp. with the intestinal barrier: Epithelium, mucus, and microbiota.
evidence.snippet: We will summarize what is known and discuss preliminary observations suggesting how such enteropathogen directly and/ or indirectly impairs commensal microbiota biofilm architecture, disrupts mucus layer and damages host epithelium physiology and survival.

evidence.reference_title: Giardiasis.
evidence.snippet: The clinical presentation typically involves diarrhea, flatulence, abdominal pain, and bloating; however, asymptomatic colonization is also common.

nodes.description: Bumetanide-sensitive secretory current is increased in chronic human giardiasis.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of chronic Giardia lamblia infection on epithelial transport and barrier function in human duodenum.
evidence.snippet: The bumetanide-sensitive portion of I(SC) in giardiasis was also increased (51 (5) vs 20 (9) microA/h/cm(2) in control; p<0.05).

nodes.description: Human duodenal giardiasis is associated with reduced epithelial resistance and barrier dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of chronic Giardia lamblia infection on epithelial transport and barrier function in human duodenum.
evidence.snippet: Instead, epithelial resistance of giardiasis biopsy specimens was decreased (19 (2) vs 25 (2) Omega cm(2); p<0.05)

evidence.reference_title: Giardiasis: nutritional implications.
evidence.snippet: Severe clinical giardiasis can cause "failure to thrive" in young children, but the impact, if any, of subclinical giardiasis on growth in general populations is not well defined.

evidence.reference_title: Mucosal Defense Against Giardia at the Intestinal Epithelial Cell Interface.
evidence.snippet: Chronic G. duodenalis infections cause a malabsorption syndrome that may lead to failure to thrive and/or stunted growth, especially in children in developing countries.

evidence.reference_title: Giardia intestinalis.
evidence.snippet: Further studies of giardiasis in poorly nourished children in developing regions supports an important contributing role of Giardia in stunting and cognitive impairment.

evidence.reference_title: The Gut-Wrenching Effects of Cryptosporidiosis and Giardiasis in Children.
evidence.snippet: Structural damages inflicted by both parasites to epithelial cells in the large and small intestines could severely impair children's gut health, including the ability to absorb nutrients, resulting in stunted growth, diminished neurocognitive development, and other long-term effects.

nodes.description: Low-oxygen conditions in infected IECs drive PKC alpha/beta II dephosphorylation linked to barrier injury.

evidence.reference_title: Oxygen-dependent regulation of permeability in low resistance intestinal epithelial cells infected with Giardia lamblia.
evidence.snippet: Collectively, these results support the emerging theory that molecular oxygen impacts gut homeostasis during Giardia infection via direct host signaling pathways.

nodes.description: Infection begins with ingestion of environmentally transmitted cysts.

evidence.reference_title: Giardiasis.
evidence.snippet: Giardiasis, caused by the protozoan parasite Giardia duodenalis, is one of the most common treatable causes of gastroenteritis worldwide, with a burden higher in low-income countries.

evidence.reference_title: Giardiasis.
evidence.snippet: In some patients, a chronic infection may lead to malabsorption and weight loss.

evidence.reference_title: Mucosal Defense Against Giardia at the Intestinal Epithelial Cell Interface.
evidence.snippet: Chronic G. duodenalis infections cause a malabsorption syndrome that may lead to failure to thrive and/or stunted growth, especially in children in developing countries.

nodes.description: Giardia impairs mucus barrier integrity at the host-microbe interface.

evidence.reference_title: Interactions of Giardia sp. with the intestinal barrier: Epithelium, mucus, and microbiota.
evidence.snippet: We will summarize what is known and discuss preliminary observations suggesting how such enteropathogen directly and/ or indirectly impairs commensal microbiota biofilm architecture, disrupts mucus layer and damages host epithelium physiology and survival.
PI-IBS was most associated with parasites (prevalence 30.1%), but in only two studies, followed by bacteria (18.3%) and viruses (10.7%).
IBS persisted in 39.8% of subjects in the long-term (>5 years follow-up) after diagnosis.

nodes.description: Sodium-dependent glucose transport is reduced in chronic human giardiasis.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of chronic Giardia lamblia infection on epithelial transport and barrier function in human duodenum.
evidence.snippet: Finally, phlorizin-sensitive Na(+)-glucose symport was reduced in patients with giardiasis (121 (9) vs 83 (14) microA/h/cm(2)).

evidence.reference_title: Giardiasis: nutritional implications.
evidence.snippet: Giardiasis can produce steatorrhea, maldigestion, and malabsorption of carbohydrates and of vitamins A and B12.

nodes.description: Giardia infection causes tight-junction disruption in intestinal epithelial cells.

evidence.reference_title: Giardia duodenalis assemblage-specific induction of apoptosis and tight junction disruption in human intestinal epithelial cells: effects of mixed infections.
evidence.snippet: The results indicate that infection with trophozoites disrupts epithelial tight junctions and induces varying degrees of enterocyte apoptosis, depending on the infecting assemblage.

nodes.description: Giardia trophozoites persist at the proximal small-intestinal lumen-epithelium interface without mucosal invasion.
nodes.id: Trophozoite colonization of the proximal small-intestinal epithelial surface

evidence.snippet: G. lamblia resides in the lumen and at the epithelial surface of the proximal small intestine but is not mucosa invasive.
